cards.definition: the main component of a computer that carries out instructions and processes data
the process the CPU repeats continuously: fetch an instruction from memory, decode it, then execute it
the part of the CPU that performs calculations and logical comparisons
the part of the CPU that coordinates all activities, directing the fetch-decode-execute cycle
tiny, very fast memory locations inside the CPU that temporarily store data being processed
a small, fast memory store inside or close to the CPU that holds frequently used data to speed up processing
the number of cycles the CPU performs per second, measured in GHz — higher means faster processing
individual processing units within a CPU — more cores allow more tasks to be processed simultaneously
a computer design where the CPU, memory, and input/output share a single bus to transfer data
a set of wires that transfers data between components of a computer
temporary, volatile memory that stores data and programs currently in use — contents lost when power is off
permanent, non-volatile memory that stores the boot instructions — cannot be changed and is not lost when power is off
memory that loses its contents when the power is switched off (e.g. RAM)
memory that keeps its contents when the power is switched off (e.g. ROM, SSD)
a section of secondary storage used as extra RAM when RAM is full — slower than real RAM
a type of non-volatile memory used in USB drives, SSDs, and memory cards — can be erased and rewritten
the program stored in ROM that runs when a computer starts up, checking hardware and loading the OS
carries the memory address of where data should be read from or written to
carries the actual data being transferred between the CPU and memory
a temporary storage area that holds data while it is being transferred between two components at different speeds
permanent storage used to save data long-term, separate from RAM (e.g. HDD, SSD, optical disc)
a magnetic storage device that uses spinning platters to read and write data — high capacity, slower than SSD
a storage device with no moving parts that uses flash memory — faster and more durable than HDD but more expensive
storage that uses laser light to read/write data on discs (e.g. CD, DVD, Blu-ray)
storage that uses magnetism to store data (e.g. HDD, magnetic tape)
storing data on remote servers accessed via the internet — accessible from anywhere but requires internet connection
the maximum amount of data a storage device can hold, measured in GB or TB
how quickly data can be saved to or retrieved from a storage device
how resistant a storage device is to physical damage — SSDs are more durable than HDDs as they have no moving parts
the delay between requesting data and receiving it — lower latency means faster access
a network connecting devices within a small geographical area such as a school or office building
a network connecting devices over a large geographical area — the internet is the largest WAN
a network where a central server provides services and resources to client devices
a network where all devices are equal and share resources directly with each other, with no central server
the maximum amount of data that can be transmitted over a network connection per second, measured in Mbps
a small chunk of data that is sent across a network — large files are split into many packets
the method of sending data by breaking it into packets that can take different routes across a network
a unique numerical label assigned to every device on a network, used to identify and locate it
a unique hardware address permanently assigned to a network interface card — used to identify devices on a LAN
a set of agreed rules that determine how data is transmitted and received across a network
a device that forwards data packets between networks and connects a LAN to the internet
a device that connects multiple devices on a LAN and forwards data only to the intended recipient device
an older device that connects devices on a LAN and sends data to all devices — less efficient than a switch
hardware that allows a device to connect to a network, either wired or wireless
a network layout where all devices connect to a central switch or hub — most common in schools and offices
a network layout where all devices connect to a single central cable — failure of the cable breaks the whole network
a network layout where every device connects to every other device — very reliable but expensive to set up
a network connection using physical cables — faster and more secure than wireless
a network connection using radio waves — convenient and flexible but slower and less secure than wired
a system that translates human-readable domain names (e.g. google.com) into IP addresses
the protocol used to transfer web pages over the internet
a secure version of HTTP that encrypts data sent between a browser and a website
the set of protocols that governs how data is sent and received across the internet
a protocol used to transfer files between computers over a network
protocols used to send (SMTP) and receive (IMAP/POP) emails across networks
the full web address used to locate a resource on the internet (e.g. https://www.bbc.co.uk)
the language used to structure and display content on web pages
a system of interlinked web pages and resources accessed via the internet using HTTP
the global network of computers and servers connected together — the WWW runs on top of the internet
storing a website or application on a server so it can be accessed over the internet
any software designed to damage, disrupt, or gain unauthorised access to a computer system
malware that attaches itself to files and spreads to other files, often causing damage
malware that replicates itself across a network without needing to attach to a file
malware disguised as legitimate software that gives an attacker access to a system
malware that encrypts a victim's files and demands payment to restore access
a social engineering attack using fake emails or websites to trick users into revealing sensitive information
an attack that systematically tries every possible password combination until the correct one is found
an attack where malicious SQL code is inserted into a web form to access or manipulate a database
an attack that floods a server with traffic to make it unavailable to legitimate users
manipulating people into revealing confidential information or performing actions that compromise security
software or hardware that monitors and controls incoming and outgoing network traffic based on security rules
the process of converting data into a coded form so only authorised parties can read it
the process of verifying a user's identity before granting access to a system
a security process requiring two different forms of verification (e.g. password + SMS code)
software that detects, quarantines, and removes malicious programs from a computer
an authorised simulated attack on a system to find and fix security vulnerabilities before real attackers do
restricting who can access certain data or systems, using usernames, passwords, and permission levels
protocols that encrypt data between a browser and a server, shown by the padlock icon in a browser
a set of rules governing acceptable use of a network — e.g. not sharing passwords, not installing unauthorised software
protecting hardware from physical access — e.g. locked server rooms, CCTV, cable locks
software that manages hardware resources and provides an interface between the user and the computer (e.g. Windows, macOS, Linux)
the OS task of allocating CPU time to running programs and managing their execution
the OS task of allocating and tracking RAM usage between programs to prevent conflicts
the OS task of organising, naming, storing, and retrieving files on storage devices
the way a user interacts with a computer — either a GUI (graphical) or CLI (command line)
an interface using windows, icons, menus, and pointers — easier to use than a CLI
an interface where users type text commands directly — harder to learn but more powerful than a GUI
the ability of an OS to run multiple programs at the same time by rapidly switching between them
software that allows the OS to communicate with a hardware device (e.g. printer driver)
programs that help maintain and manage a computer system (e.g. antivirus, disk defragmenter, backup tools)
a UK law making it illegal to access computer systems without permission, spread malware, or commit fraud using computers
a UK law controlling how personal data is collected, stored, and used by organisations
a UK law protecting the intellectual property of creators — making illegal copies is an offence
creations of the mind (software, music, writing) that are legally protected from copying or theft
software whose source code is freely available for anyone to view, use, and modify
software owned by a company whose source code is not publicly available — users must pay a licence fee
software that is free to use but whose source code is not available (unlike open source)
the gap between people who have access to technology and the internet and those who do not
the effect of technology on the environment — including energy use, e-waste, and carbon emissions from data centres
issues raised by artificial intelligence including bias, job displacement, privacy, and accountability
a precise, step-by-step set of instructions to solve a problem or complete a task
a diagram using shapes to represent the steps of an algorithm — ovals for start/end, rectangles for processes, diamonds for decisions
an informal way of describing an algorithm using plain English and programming-like structure, without exact syntax
breaking a large, complex problem down into smaller, more manageable sub-problems
removing unnecessary detail from a problem to focus only on the essential information needed to solve it
identifying similarities or common features in problems to help find solutions more efficiently
an approach to problem-solving that involves decomposition, abstraction, pattern recognition, and algorithm design
a basic model of computing: data goes in (input), something happens to it (process), and a result comes out (output)
how well an algorithm uses time and memory — a more efficient algorithm solves the same problem faster or using less memory
a table used to manually track the values of variables as an algorithm runs, step by step — used to test and debug
a search algorithm that checks each item in a list one by one from the start until the target is found or the list ends
a fast search algorithm that repeatedly halves a sorted list to find a target — much faster than linear search on large lists
a sorting algorithm that repeatedly compares adjacent items and swaps them if in the wrong order — simple but slow on large lists
a sorting algorithm that splits a list in half repeatedly, sorts each half, then merges them back together — efficient on large lists
a sorting algorithm that builds a sorted list one item at a time by inserting each new item in the correct position
a measure of how the running time of an algorithm grows as the size of the input increases
a way of expressing how the time or space requirements of an algorithm scale with input size (e.g. O(n), O(n²))
an algorithm strategy that splits a problem into smaller sub-problems, solves them, then combines the results
a condition that must be true before an algorithm can run correctly — e.g. binary search requires a sorted list
manually stepping through an algorithm on paper to check it works correctly before running it on a computer
a named storage location in a program that holds a value which can change during execution
a named value in a program that is set once and cannot be changed during execution
the type of value a variable holds — e.g. integer (whole number), float (decimal), string (text), boolean (true/false)
instructions executed one after another in order — the most basic programming construct
a programming construct that allows different code to run depending on whether a condition is true or false
a programming construct that repeats a block of code — either a set number of times (for loop) or while a condition is true (while loop)
a loop that repeats a set number of times — used when you know in advance how many repetitions are needed
a loop that continues repeating as long as a condition remains true — used when the number of repetitions is not known in advance
a named, reusable block of code that performs a specific task — can be called multiple times from different parts of a program
a value passed into a function when it is called, allowing the function to work with different data each time
a data structure that stores multiple values of the same type in a single named list, accessed by an index number
the position number used to access an item in an array — in most languages, indexing starts at 0
a sequence of characters (letters, numbers, symbols) stored as text
built-in functions to manipulate strings — e.g. length, substring, upper/lower case, concatenation
a data structure that stores related data of different types together (like a row in a database)
the ability of a program to open, read data from, write data to, and close files stored on a computer
a variable declared inside a function — only accessible within that function
a variable declared outside functions — accessible from anywhere in the program but can cause errors if misused
converting a value from one data type to another — e.g. converting a string '5' to an integer 5
joining two or more strings together end-to-end (e.g. 'Hello' + ' ' + 'World' = 'Hello World')
an error caused by code that breaks the rules of the programming language — the program will not run
an error where the program runs but produces the wrong output — the code is valid but the logic is incorrect
an error that causes a program to crash while it is running — e.g. dividing by zero or accessing an invalid array index
the process of finding and fixing errors in a program
data used to test whether a program produces the correct output — should include normal, boundary, and erroneous values
typical valid inputs that the program should handle correctly
values at the edges of acceptable input ranges — used to test if the program handles limits correctly
invalid inputs that the program should reject — used to test error handling
testing a program repeatedly throughout development, fixing errors as they are found
testing a completed program to check it meets the original requirements before it is released
the smallest unit of data in a computer — a single binary digit, either 0 or 1
a group of 8 bits — the standard unit of data storage
a number system using only 0s and 1s — the language computers use to store and process all data
the standard number system humans use, with digits 0–9
a number system using digits 0–9 and letters A–F — used as a compact way to represent binary data
adding binary numbers together — key rule: 1 + 1 = 10 in binary (carry the 1)
moving all bits left or right — a left shift multiplies by 2, a right shift divides by 2
a method of representing negative numbers in binary — invert all bits then add 1
an error that occurs when a calculation produces a result too large to be stored in the available number of bits
a standard that assigns a unique binary code to each character (letters, numbers, symbols) so text can be stored digitally
the smallest element of a digital image — each pixel stores a colour value
the number of pixels in an image — higher resolution means more detail but larger file size
the number of bits used to represent each pixel's colour — more bits = more possible colours
calculated as: image width × image height × colour depth (in bits), then divided by 8 for bytes
the number of times per second a sound wave is measured when recording digital audio — higher rate = better quality
the number of bits used to record each audio sample — higher bit depth = more accurate sound reproduction
compression that reduces file size without losing any data — the original file can be perfectly restored (e.g. PNG, ZIP)
compression that reduces file size by permanently removing some data — smaller files but lower quality (e.g. MP3, JPEG)
a lossless compression technique that replaces repeated data with a count and value (e.g. AAAA → 4A)
a character encoding standard that covers characters from all world languages — an extension of ASCII
